content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444


January 6, 1984


Donald K. Rothenbaum
8908 New Dawn Drive
Sacramento, CA  95826

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-83-278

Dear Mr. Rothenbaum:

     This letter is sent in response to copies of letters we
received from Barbara V. Carr, Chief, Personnel Services Section
of the Department of Health Services, and Raymond V. Brock,
Director of Project Development, of Chemical Waste Management,
Inc., who are requesting advice on your behalf.  We have been
asked to determine the effect of the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act/ should you accept a
job with Chemical Waste Management, Inc.  My understanding of
the situation from the letters and from a telephone conversation
with Ms. Nancy Skinner of the Personnel Services Section of the
Department of Health Services is as follows.

     You are currently employed as a Waste Management
Specialist II with the California Department of Health Services
("DHS") in the Site Cleanup and Emergency Response Section of
the Hazardous Waste Site Cleanup Unit.  According to a duty
statement attached to our copy of Ms. Carr's letter, your duties
and responsibilities are to:

     Provide technical and analytical support and consultation
regarding methods and procedures for evaluating specific
site cleanup actions;

_____________________

1/  Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.  All statutory
references are to the California Government Code.  This office
interprets and advises only as to the application of the
Political Reform Act; there may be other state laws and policies
which are pertinent to this situation.

Donald K. Rothenbaum
January 6, 1984
Page 2


Maintain accurate numerical, technical and narrative records
concerning actions taken at individual waste disposal sites
along with summary documentation of all data in a manner
suitable for presentation in cases of litigation;

Review technical materials and reports requiring knowledge
of geology, hydrology, chemistry and related environmental
sciences and advise program management as to appropriate
findings, conclusions and recommendations;

Participate as a technical support field person for on-site
inspections;

Act as an expert witness in litigation;

Perform administrative and other program duties as required.

I understand from Nancy Skinner that you are integrally
involved in the contract process for the clean up of hazardous
waste dump sites. You make specific recommendations on the
what, how and when of a clean up; review the actual bid
proposals, and make substantive recommendations on their
adequacy. As part of your duties, you have been involved in the
McColl Site Project and the Aerojet Project. Both of these
projects entail the clean up of hazardous waste dump sites for
which DOHS will award state contracts.

You were recently offered a management position in the
Environmental Remedial Action Division of Chemical Waste
Management, Inc. (the "company"). This company bids for state
and Federal contracts under superfund statutes to clean up
emergency hazardous waste situations and abandoned hazardous
waste dump sites. The company is presently preparing a contract
did proposal to the State of California for the McColl Site
Project in Fullerton, California. The bid proposal will be
reviewed, and the contract awarded, by DOHS.

The question raised by both Ms. Carr and Mr. Brock is,
should you accept the job with the company, whether the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act will limit or
curtail your activities with the company in any way.

The provisions of the Act which apply to former state
employees who work in the private sector are Sections 87400
through 87405. The basic prohibition is found in Section 87401
which provides as follows:

Donald K. Rothenbaum
January 6, 1984
Page 3


No former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office,
shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or
otherwise represent, any other person (other than the
State of California) before any court or state
administrative agency or any officer or employee
thereof:

(a) By making any formal or informal appearance,
or by making any oral or written communication with the
intent to influence, in connection with any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding if;

(b) The State of California is a party or has a
direct and substantial interest; and

(c) If the proceeding is one in which the former
state administrative official participated.

As a Waste Management Specialist II, you are a "state
administrative official" within the meaning of Section 87401,
Section 87400(b). Matters concerning contracts are included in
the definition of "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding." Section 87400(c).2/ Accordingly, if you take the
position with the company, you may not make any attempts to
influence a decision of DOHS with regard to any matter in which
you have participated as a state employee. Section
87400(d).3/ Thus, for example, since you have participated in
DOHS decisions on the McColl Site Project (e.g., worked on the
Request for Proposal or analyzed the specifications for clean up
of the site), you may not, in your position with the company,

_________________________

2/ Section 87400(c) defines "judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding" to mean "any proceeding, application, request
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim,
controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other
particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any
court or state administrative agency...."

3/ Section 87400(d) defines "participated" to mean "to
have taken part personally and substantially through decision,
approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering
advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of
confidential information as an officer or employee...."

Donald K. Rothenbaum
January 6, 1984
Page 4


make any attempts to influence DOHS in its handling of the
matter. Attempting to influence DOHS would include making any
type of appearance before the agency or making any type of
written or oral communication to the agency made with the intent
to influence its decision making.

In addition to the prohibition in Section 87401, a former
state employee may not "aid, advise, counsel, consult or assist
in representing any other person in any proceeding in which the
official would be prohibited from appearing under Section
87401." Section 87402. This means that, if you take the
position with the company, you may not advise or assist the
company concerning any matter pending before DOHS in which you
participated as a state employee, including the McColl Site
Project. You should review your activities at DOHS carefully to
determine which other matters, if any, are covered by the
prohibitions in Sections 87401 and 87402.

There are three exemptions to the prohibitions contained in
Sections 87401 and 87402:

     (a)  To prevent a former state administrative
official from making or providing a statement, which is
based on the former state administrative official's own
special knowledge in the particular area that is the
subject of the statement, provided that no compensation
is thereby received other than that regularly provided
for by law or regulation for witnesses; or

     (b)  To communications made solely for the purpose
of furnishing information by a former state
administrative official if the court or state
administrative agency to which the communication is
directed makes findings in writing that:

          (1)  The former state administrative official has
outstanding and otherwise unavailable qualifications;

          (2)  The former state administrative official is
acting with respect to a particular matter which
requires such qualifications; and

          (3)  The public interest would be served by the
participation of the former state administrative
official; or

Donald K. Rotnenbaum
January 6, 1984
Page 5


     (c)  With respect to appearances or communications
in a proceeding in which a court or state
administrative agency has issued a final order, decree,
decision or judgment but has retained jurisdiction if
the state administrative agency of former employment
gives its consent by determining that:

     (1)  At least five years have elapsed since the
termination of the former state administrative
official's employment or term of office; and

     (2)  The public interest would not be harmed.

Section 87403.

None of these exemptions appear relevant to your situation.

     I trust that the foregoing discussion has been helpful to
you.  If I can be of further assistance or if you would like to
discuss this letter, please feel free to call me at
(916) 322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:plh

STATE OF CALIFORNIA—HEALTH AND WELFARE AGENCY                                GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH SERVICES
714.744 P STREET
SACRAMENTO CA 95814
(916) 445-6678                                                December 9, 1983




Donald K. Rothenbaum
3908 New Dawn Drive
Sacramento, CA 95826

Dear Mr. Rothenbaum:

     This is in response to your letter of November 18, 1983 requesting assistance
in determining whether a conflict of interest may exist should you obtain em-
ployment with Chemical Waste Management, Inc.

     As Nancy Skinner of my staff informed you on December 5, 1983, it appears that
no conflict of interest would exist if you should terminate your employment
with the Department of Health Services and secure employment with Chemical
Waste Management, Inc., provided that you are not involved, in any fashion,
which includes contract bids on the McColl Site Project or any other projects
that you may have worked on within the last five years, while employed by the
State of California. Since the California conflict of interest laws provide
for certain exemptions, we have requested Fair Political Practices Commission
to communicate directly to you, as well as to Mr. Raymond Bock of the Chemical
Waste Management firm, the specific details of those restricted activities
for former State employees. You should be in receipt of this additional in-
formation within two to three weeks.

     Should you have additional questions, please contact me at (916) 445-6678 or
Nancy Skinner at (916) 445-1154.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara V. Carr, Chief
                                        Personnel Services Section

cc: Barbara Milman
    General Counsel, Legal Division
    Fair Political Practices Commission
    1100 K Street
    Sacramento, CA 95804

    Raymond W. Brock
    Director of Project Development
    Chemical Waste Management, Inc.
    3003 Butterfield Road
    Oak Brook, IL 60521

JOB POSITION

Site Management Specialist II
C-a Manager and Envirnnmental Specialist (Initial
Classified Codes V-1 Programs of C.

Under the direct supervision of a Senior Waste Management Engineer or Waste
Management Specialist I in the Project [illegible], the waste
is a [illegible] [illegible].

II. Provide technical and analytical support regarding methods and procedures recording
methods and procedures for evaluating and monitoring hazardous wastes;

IV. Utilize in advanced numerical, formulas and chemical reports from
chemical facilities under individual watch or plans, with summary documentation of all data in a manner otherwise for presentation
or cases of litigation;

V. Utilize technical materials and priority with working knowledge of geology,
hydrology, chemistry and related environmental sciences and advise as
program management as to appropriate findings, conclusions and recommendations;

VI. Participate as a technical support field screen for on-site inspections;

VII. Act as an expert witness in litigation;

VIII. Perform administrative and other program duties as required.

[illegible text at bottom of page]

November 17, 1983


Barbara Carr
Chief of Personnel Services Section
Personnel Management Branch
Department of Health Services
714 P Street - Room 850
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ms. Carr:

I am writing to you in order to obtain an official response from the State
of California-DOHS as it relates to the following circumstances:

1.    Mr. Don Rothenbaum, who is a current employee of the State of Cali-
      fornia-DOHS, contacted our company and inquired whether there were
      any positions available within our company since he had made the
      decision that he was going to leave his current position at the State.

2.    Upon review of Mr. Rothenbaum's qualifications, background and experi-
      ence, we made the determination that he would be a good candidate for a
      position within our company.

3.    The position that we would offer to Mr. Rothenbaum would be a management
      position within our ENRAC (Environmental Remedial Action) Division.

4.    Mr. Rothenbaum's current duties for the State directly involves him in
      the area of environmental management/control that our ENRAC Division
      is also associated with, namely clean-up of emergency situations and
      abandoned dump sites under State and Federal Superfund statutes.

5.    We are aware of conflict of interest stipulations and statutes that
      are part of the State of California bidding procedures as they relate
      to former State employees working for companies that bid for State
      contracts.

6.    As a potential bidder to the State on such projects as mentioned in 4.
      above, we feel it is essential to receive an official determination
      from the State whether the hiring of Mr. Rothenbaum would act to
      disqualify or in any other way negatively affect our company in the
      bidding process to the State. In order for the State to respond
      adequately, we must clarify that, if we make an offer to Mr. Rothenbaum
      to come to work for our company and if Mr. Rothenbaum accepts the
      position, our company would not allow Mr. Rothenbaum to be involved in

- 2 -

any fashion with any projects that are sponsored by or being bid by the
State of California-DOHS for a period of at least one year. We would,
in fact, take additional steps to isolate Mr. Rothenbaum from all such
projects.

On the basis of the foregoing, we would like to receive a definitive deter-
mination from the State of California-DOHS whether or not the hiring of Mr.
Rothenbaum, under the conditions explained, will, in any way, effect Chemical
Waste Management, Inc. or our ENRAC Division in our submission of bids to
the State. This is particularly important in light of the fact that we are
currently preparing a bid response to the State for the McColl Project in
Fullerton, California.

We will sincerely appreciate your attention and written response to this
matter at the earliest possible date. If you should have any questions,
please do not hesitate to contact me.

Very truly yours,

Raymond W. Bock
Director of Project Development

RWB/bf

Mr. Donald K. Fornenbaum
6003 New Dawn Drive
Sacramento, CA 95826

NOV 1 8 1983

Ms. Barbara Carr
Personnel Officer
Department of Health Services
714 P Street, Room 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

November 18, 1983

Dear Ms. Carr:

As we discussed, I am in consideration for a job with Chemical Waste Management
Incorporated, a Chicago based firm. The job and of course any offer would be
contingent on resolving any concerns regarding conflict of interest. The back-
ground which raises this concern is as follows and is as comprehensive and as
accurate as I can present:

From approximately November 1982 to June 1983, I was assigned to work on the
McColl Hazardous Waste Site in Fullerton, Orange County. My responsibility was
technical in nature and involved coordination among involved agency and industry
representatives. All management and policy decisions were made by the Section
Supervisor, Mr. Thomas Bailey. In addition, direction and input on major de-
cisions were provided by a project committee consisting of local and state
agencies and three oil companies. No decision was ever arbitrarily made by
this Department.

Technical assistance to the Department and the project committee was provided
by Radian Corporation, a Texas based consulting firm. Chemical Waste Management
was involved in the project but that involvement was limited to a test excavation
conducted at the site during May of this year. Chemical Waste was a subcontractor
to Radian Corporation, the primary contractor on this project. In addition the funding for
Radian's and therefore Chemical Waste's work on the test excavations was provided
by one of the oil companies on the project committee. To date, Chemical Waste
Management has not been hired by the State for any portion of the project.

Since my involvement in this project terminated in June of this year I have had
no involvement in the project or with the company. The project has since pro-
gressed through design and is now out for bid. Chemical Waste is one of many
bidding for this project. I have had no direct involvement or verbal input into
the preparation of the bid package, selection procedures/criteria and will not
have any dealings in evaluation of the bids or selection of the contractor.

The job with Chemical Waste Management, although it has not been presented to me
in any detail, would be as a program director for their Northern California
operation. It would not involve any work on McColl in the event they do acquire

Ms. Barbara Carr                                    -2-




the contract as it would be handled by their Southern California operation.

I have presented the above to my Division's personnel officer, Ms. Joanne Eve.
She has inquired with Mr. Joe Symkowick, Chief Counsel for the Toxics Division.
It was his opinion that no conflict of interest was involved. In addition,
Chemical Waste Management should be contacting you to discuss this from a
broader perspective as it relates to any future state contract work for
remedial projects.

I am very interested in working for Chemical Waste Management but job prospects
cannot be discussed until the conflict of interest issue is resolved. I would
greatly appreciate an official opinion on this matter at your earliest convenience.

Thank you in advance.

Sincerely,


[signature]
Donald. K. Rothenbaum
Home: (916) 363-0745
Work: (916) 324-1732

cc: Thomas E. Bailey
    Ms. Joanne Eve
    Mr. Joe Symkowick
    Mr. Ray Bock, Chemical
    Waste Management Inc.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a former California state employee (Waste Management Specialist II at the Department of Health Services) would violate conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act if he accepts a management position with Chemical Waste Management, Inc., a company bidding on state contracts for hazardous waste cleanup projects that he previously worked on.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The former employee is prohibited under Sections 87401 and 87402 from making any attempts to influence the Department of Health Services regarding matters in which he participated as a state employee, including the McColl Site Project. He cannot advise or assist the private company on any pending matter before DOHS in which he previously participated. No available exemptions apply to his situation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes three separate letters: the primary FPPC advice letter (dated January 6, 1984) from Diane Maura Fishburn, a preliminary letter from DOHS (December 9, 1983) referring the matter to FPPC, and an incoming request letter from Chemical Waste Management (November 17, 1983). Extracted data focuses on the FPPC advice letter as the primary document. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present; content synthesized from narrative discussion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a former California state employee (Waste Management Specialist II at the Department of Health Services) would violate conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act if he accepts a management position with Chemical Waste Management, Inc., a company bidding on state contracts for hazardous waste cleanup projects that he previously worked on.

CONCLUSION: The former employee is prohibited under Sections 87401 and 87402 from making any attempts to influence the Department of Health Services regarding matters in which he participated as a state employee, including the McColl Site Project. He cannot advise or assist the private company on any pending matter before DOHS in which he previously participated. No available exemptions apply to his situation.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a request regarding conflict of interest restrictions for a state employee (Waste Management Specialist II at DOHS) considering employment with a private contractor (Chemical Waste Management, Inc.) that bids on state hazardous waste cleanup contracts. The letter explains that Political Reform Act Sections 87401-87402 prohibit the former employee from influencing DOHS decisions or advising the company on projects he worked on, particularly the McColl Site Project.
